content:
# Acts Chapter 11 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Acts 11 (Peter's Report to Jerusalem, Church at Antioch, Agabus Famine Prophecy)

## Critical Textual Issues

### 1. Verse 20 -- Hellenistas (ELLHNISTAS) vs. Hellenas (ELLHNAS)

**Severity:** MAJOR -- This is one of the most debated textual variants in Acts.

**The readings:**
- **Sinaiticus (Aleph):** ELLHNISTAS (Hellenistas -- "Hellenists," Greek-speaking people)
- **Variant in other manuscripts:** ELLHNAS (Hellenas -- "Greeks," ethnic Gentiles)

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** Renders "Greeks" but notes Sinaiticus reads Hellenistas
- **LOGOS:** Renders "Greeks" and notes Hellenas as the reading (appears to follow a different manuscript tradition for the text while using Sinaiticus as base)
- **PNEUMA:** Renders "Greeks" but notes Sinaiticus reads Hellenistas

**The problem:** Hellenistas normally means "Greek-speaking Jews" in Acts (cf. 6:1, 9:29). But the context of v. 20 contrasts this new audience with the "Jews only" preaching of v. 19, which demands that a different group is being reached. If Hellenistas is original, either (a) Luke uses the term in an expanded sense here to include Greek-speaking Gentiles, or (b) a very early scribal error confused the similar words. If Hellenas is original, a scribe may have assimilated it to the more common Lukan term Hellenistas.

**SOPHIA resolution:** The SOPHIA Bible preserves "Hellenists" as the Sinaiticus reading, consistent with our governing principle of manuscript fidelity. The narrative context makes the intended sense (non-Jewish audience) unmistakable. Both EMET and PNEUMA correctly identify the Sinaiticus reading as Hellenistas. This is a case where the lectio difficilior (harder reading) may well be original -- a scribe would be more likely to change the confusing Hellenistas to the clearer Hellenas than the reverse.

---

### 2. Verse 6 -- Inclusion of "Wild Beasts" (kai ta thēria)

**Severity:** MODERATE -- Affects the relationship between Peter's retelling and the original narrative.

**The issue:** In Acts 10:12 (the original vision narrative), Sinaiticus lists three categories of creatures: four-footed animals, creeping things, and birds. Here in Acts 11:6 (Peter's retelling), Sinaiticus includes a fourth category: "wild beasts" (kai ta thēria).

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** Includes wild beasts; notes the addition is "distinctive of Peter's retelling"
- **LOGOS:** Includes wild beasts
- **PNEUMA:** Includes wild beasts

**SOPHIA resolution:** All three translators agree. The four-category list is preserved. The addition of "wild beasts" in the retelling may reflect Peter's rhetorical emphasis (emphasizing the shocking uncleanness of the vision's contents before a skeptical audience) or may preserve a fuller tradition of the vision. Either way, it is the Sinaiticus reading and all translators follow it.

---

### 3. Verse 12 -- diakrinanta: "Making No Distinction" vs. "Doubting Nothing" vs. "Without Hesitation"

**Severity:** MODERATE -- Theological nuance at stake.

**The Greek:** mēden diakrinanta -- accusative singular aorist participle of diakrinō

**The semantic range of diakrinō:**
- To separate, distinguish, discriminate
- To judge, evaluate
- To doubt, hesitate (middle voice sense)

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** "doubting nothing" -- emphasizes the psychological sense (hesitation)
- **LOGOS:** "making no distinction" -- emphasizes the social/theological sense (discrimination)
- **PNEUMA:** "without hesitation" -- dynamic rendering of the doubt sense

**SOPHIA resolution:** "Making no distinction" -- follows LOGOS. The theological weight tips the balance. The same root (diakrinō) appears in v. 2 where the circumcision party "contended" (diekrinonto) with Peter. The Spirit's command to Peter directly counters the very act his critics are performing: they draw distinctions between Jew and Gentile; the Spirit told Peter to make no such distinction. This verbal echo is central to the chapter's argument and is preserved only by the "distinction" rendering.

---

### 4. Verse 2 -- "Circumcision Party" Terminology

**Severity:** MINOR -- Stylistic variation.

**The Greek:** hoi ek peritomēs -- "those of/from the circumcision"

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** "those of [the] circumcision" -- literal partitive
- **LOGOS:** "those of the circumcision" -- formal equivalent
- **PNEUMA:** "those of the circumcision party" -- adds "party" to clarify factional identity

**SOPHIA resolution:** "Those of the circumcision" -- follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1). The Greek does not include a word for "party," though the partitive construction (hoi ek) does imply a defined group. SOPHIA preserves the Greek phrasing and lets the context convey the factional sense. The addition of "party" is interpretive, however defensible.

---

### 5. Verse 26 -- "Christians" (Christianous) and chrēmatisai

**Severity:** MINOR -- All three translators agree on substance; nuance of the verb differs.

**The Greek:** chrēmatisai te prōton en Antiocheia tous mathētas Christianous

**The verb chrēmatizō can mean:**
- To transact business, deal with
- To bear a name publicly, be called
- To be divinely warned or directed (cf. Matt 2:12, 2:22; Acts 10:22)

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** "the disciples were called Christians first in Antioch" -- notes the public/official connotation
- **LOGOS:** "the disciples were first called Christians in Antioch" -- notes possible divine appointment sense
- **PNEUMA:** "the disciples were first called Christians" -- notes the identity-forming moment

**SOPHIA resolution:** "The disciples were first called Christians in Antioch" -- all three essentially agree. The passive voice of chrēmatisai leaves the agency ambiguous: outsiders may have coined the name (as a label for "Christ-followers"), or it may carry overtones of divine designation. SOPHIA preserves the ambiguity by retaining the passive construction.

---

### 6. Verse 28 -- "Whole Inhabited World" (holēn tēn oikoumenēn)

**Severity:** MINOR -- Translation scope question.

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** "the whole inhabited-earth"
- **LOGOS:** "the whole inhabited world"
- **PNEUMA:** "the entire Roman world"

**SOPHIA resolution:** "The whole inhabited world" -- follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's "Roman world" is historically accurate (oikoumenē in first-century usage referred to the Roman Empire) but imports an interpretive specification absent from the Greek. The historical note "under Claudius" provides sufficient context for the reader. The Josephus-attested famine of AD 46-47 confirms the geographical scope.

---

### 7. Verse 11 -- "In Which I Was" vs. "In Which We Were"

**Severity:** MINOR -- Pronoun reading in the manuscript.

**Translator positions:**
- **EMET:** "in which I was" (ēmēn -- first person singular)
- **LOGOS:** "in which we were" (ēmen -- first person plural)
- **PNEUMA:** "where I was staying" (first person singular, dynamic)

**SOPHIA resolution:** "In which I was" -- follows EMET. The Sinaiticus reading HMHN corresponds to ēmēn (first person singular imperfect of eimi). Peter is recounting his personal experience in first-person narrative. The first person singular is the expected form in this context. LOGOS may have followed a variant or misread the form; the difference between HMHN and HMEN in uncial script is minimal (a single stroke).

---

## Full Disagreement Table

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Word order / emphasis | "also the Gentiles received" | "the Gentiles also had received" | "the Gentiles too had received" | "the Gentiles also had received" -- follows LOGOS |
| 2 | Verb for confrontation | "were disputing with him" | "contended with him" | "challenged him" | "contended with him" -- follows LOGOS; preserves diakrino root |
| 3 | "men having uncircumcision" vs. "uncircumcised men" | "men having uncircumcision" | "uncircumcised men" | "uncircumcised men" | "uncircumcised men" -- 2-to-1 |
| 4 | Opening phrasing | "And Peter having begun was explaining" | "But Peter began and explained" | "Peter began and laid the whole thing out" | "But Peter began and explained" -- follows LOGOS |
| 5 | "ecstasy" vs. "trance" | "in an ecstasy" | "in a trance" | "fell into a trance" | "in a trance" -- consistent with Acts 10:10 |
| 5 | "linen-sheet" vs. "sheet" | "a great linen-sheet" | "a great sheet" | "a great sheet" | "a great sheet" -- 2-to-1 |
| 6 | Wild beasts included | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes -- all agree; Sinaiticus four-category list |
| 6 | "creeping-things" vs. "reptiles" | "creeping-things" | "creeping things" | "reptiles" | "creeping things" -- 2-to-1 |
| 8 | "By no means" vs. "Absolutely not" | "By no means" | "By no means" | "Absolutely not" | "By no means" -- 2-to-1 |
| 8 | "common or unclean" vs. "impure or unclean" | "common or unclean" | "common or unclean" | "impure or unclean" | "common or unclean" -- 2-to-1 |
| 9 | "make common" vs. "call common" vs. "call impure" | "you shall not make common" | "you must not call common" | "you must not call impure" | "you must not call common" -- follows LOGOS |
| 11 | "I was" vs. "we were" | "in which I was" | "in which we were" | "where I was staying" | "in which I was" -- follows EMET; Sinaiticus HMHN = 1st person singular |
| 12 | **diakrino rendering** | "doubting nothing" | "making no distinction" | "without hesitation" | **"making no distinction"** -- follows LOGOS; theological precision; verbal echo with v. 2 |
| 13 | "send-for" vs. "summon" vs. "bring back" | "send-for" | "summon" | "bring back" | "summon" -- follows LOGOS |
| 17 | "equal gift" vs. "same gift" | "the equal gift" | "the same gift" | "the same gift" | "the same gift" -- 2-to-1 |
| 17 | "hinder God" phrasing | "who was I [to be] able to hinder God?" | "who was I to be able to hinder God?" | "who was I to stand in God's way?" | "who was I to be able to hinder God?" -- follows EMET/LOGOS |
| 18 | "repentance unto life" vs. dynamic | "repentance unto life" | "repentance unto life" | "the chance to turn to him and live" | "repentance unto life" -- 2-to-1; preserves theological formula |
| 19 | "tribulation" vs. "persecution" | "tribulation" | "tribulation" | "persecution" | "tribulation" -- 2-to-1; thlipsis, not diōgmos |
| 20 | **Hellenistas vs. Hellenas** | "Greeks" (notes Hellenistas in ms.) | "Greeks" (notes Hellenas) | "Greeks" (notes Hellenistas in ms.) | **"Hellenists"** -- preserves Sinaiticus ELLHNISTAS; lectio difficilior; see detailed discussion above |
| 22 | "word was heard" vs. "report came" | "the word was heard in the ears" | "the report concerning them came to the ears" | "News of this reached the ears" | "the report concerning them came to the ears" -- follows LOGOS |
| 23 | "purpose of heart" vs. "resolute heart" | "with purpose of heart" | "with resolute heart" | "with unwavering hearts" | "with resolute heart" -- follows LOGOS |
| 24 | "considerable crowd" vs. "large crowd" | "a considerable crowd" | "a considerable crowd" | "a large crowd" | "a considerable crowd" -- 2-to-1 |
| 25 | "went out" vs. "departed" | "he went out" | "he departed" | "Barnabas went" | "he departed" -- follows LOGOS |
| 28 | "inhabited-earth" vs. "inhabited world" vs. "Roman world" | "the whole inhabited-earth" | "the whole inhabited world" | "the entire Roman world" | "the whole inhabited world" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA imports interpretive gloss |
| 28 | "was signifying" vs. "indicated" vs. "predicted" | "was signifying" | "indicated" | "predicted" | "indicated" -- follows LOGOS |
| 29 | "for ministry" vs. "relief" | "for ministry" | "relief" | "relief" | "relief" -- 2-to-1; diakonia here = material aid |
| 29 | Prosperity phrasing | "according as any was prospering" | "according as anyone had the means" | "each according to their ability" | "according as anyone had the means" -- follows LOGOS |
| 30 | "having sent" vs. "sending it" | "having sent to the elders" | "sending it to the elders" | "sending their gift to the elders" | "sending it to the elders" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "gift" |

-- SOPHIA
